questions.question:
A computer system has 6 tape devices, with n processes competing for them. Each process may need 3 tape drives. The maximum value of n for which the system is guaranteed to be deadlock-free is:
A. 2
B. 3
C. 4
D. 1
questions.to_find: Maximum number of processes n for which the system is guaranteed to be deadlock-free

understand.plain: We have 6 tape drives shared among n processes. Each process may need at most 3 tape drives. We want to find the largest n such that no deadlock can ever happen, regardless of timing. Deadlock occurs when every process is holding some resources and waiting for more, with none able to proceed.

keywords.explain: A system is guaranteed deadlock-free if, even in the worst case (all processes simultaneously holding their maximum minus one resources), at least one process can still get all it needs and finish.
keywords.example: If 2 processes each hold 2 tape drives (max-1=2), that uses 4 of 6, leaving 2 free — enough for one process to get its 3rd drive and complete.

keywords.explain: The worst case for deadlock is when every process holds (max_need - 1) resources simultaneously — each is one resource short of finishing but won't release what it holds.
keywords.example: Each process holds 2 tape drives but needs 1 more to finish; if no free drives exist, all are stuck.

understand.visual_alt: 6 tape drives distributed among n processes, each needing at most 3. In the worst case each process holds 2 drives (max-1), leaving a remainder that may allow one process to complete.

given.aim: Find maximum n such that deadlock is impossible with 6 tape drives and max demand 3 per process.

terms.meaning: Total number of tape drive resources available in the system.
terms.connects: This is the total pool from which all processes draw resources.

terms.meaning: Each process needs at most 3 tape drives to complete its task.
terms.example: Process P1 may request 1, 2, or 3 tape drives.
terms.connects: Determines the worst-case hold scenario: each process may hold max_need - 1 = 2 drives.

terms.meaning: n*(max_need - 1) + 1 <= R ensures that even if every process holds max-1 resources, there is still 1 free resource to let some process complete.

given.plan: Apply the deadlock-free condition: n*(max_need - 1) + 1 <= R. Substitute R=6 and max_need=3, then solve for the maximum integer n.

steps.title: State the deadlock-free condition
steps.apply: For a system with R resources and max demand k per process, the condition guaranteeing no deadlock is: n*(k-1) + 1 <= R. This ensures that even when all n processes simultaneously hold k-1 resources each, the remaining 1 resource can satisfy at least one process to completion.
steps.note: This comes from the fact that if all processes hold (k-1) resources and we have at least 1 extra, that 1 extra can go to any process, letting it complete and release its (k-1) held resources, unblocking the rest.

steps.title: Substitute values and solve
steps.apply: R = 6, max_need = 3. So: n*(3-1) + 1 <= 6 => 2n + 1 <= 6 => 2n <= 5 => n <= 2.5. Since n must be an integer, maximum n = 2.
steps.note: With n=2: each process holds at most 2 drives => worst case 4 drives used, 2 remain free. One process can get its 3rd drive, complete, and release. With n=3: worst case 6 drives used, 0 free => deadlock possible.

questions.formula_note: Deadlock-free resource bound: n*(max_need - 1) + 1 <= R, which guarantees at least one process can always make progress.

questions.question:
Consider a system having m resources of the same type. These resources are shared by 3 processes A, B, and C which have peak demands of 3, 4, and 6 respectively. For what value of m, deadlock will not occur?
A. 7
B. 9
C. 10
D. 13
questions.to_find: Minimum value of m (total resources) for which deadlock is guaranteed not to occur

understand.plain: Three processes share resources of a single type. Their maximum needs are 3, 4, and 6. We want to know how many total resources m must be available so that no deadlock can ever happen. A deadlock happens when each process holds some resources and waits for more, with none able to proceed.

keywords.explain: The maximum number of resources a process might need at any point during its execution.
keywords.example: Process C with peak demand 6 may request up to 6 resources.

keywords.explain: The minimum total resources such that for any allocation, the system can always find a safe sequence. Formula: m >= sum(max_i) - n + 1, where n is number of processes.

understand.visual_alt: Three processes A (max 3), B (max 4), C (max 6) sharing m resources of one type. Need to find the minimum m preventing deadlock.

given.aim: Find the minimum value of m for which deadlock is guaranteed not to occur with 3 processes having peak demands 3, 4, 6.

terms.meaning: Three processes A, B, C compete for the same type of resource.
terms.example: A, B, C run concurrently and may each hold and request resources.
terms.connects: The number of processes determines how many can simultaneously be in a wait state.

terms.meaning: Process A needs at most 3, B at most 4, C at most 6 resources.
terms.example: In the worst case, A holds 2, B holds 3, C holds 5 (each holds max-1).
terms.connects: Worst-case hold determines total resources consumed before deadlock.

terms.meaning: m >= sum(max_i - 1) + 1 = sum(max_i) - n + 1. This ensures at least one free resource exists even when all processes hold max-1.
terms.example: m >= (2+3+5)+1 = 11, so m >= 11 guarantees no deadlock.
terms.connects: Gives the minimum m that ensures deadlock freedom.

given.plan: Apply the deadlock-free condition: m >= sum(max_i) - n + 1. With sum = 3+4+6 = 13 and n = 3: m >= 11. Check which option satisfies this.

steps.title: Identify worst-case allocation
steps.apply: In the worst case for deadlock, every process holds exactly (max_need - 1) resources and waits for one more. Total resources consumed in this state: 2+3+5 = 10. With m=10, all resources are held and no process can proceed => deadlock.
steps.note: With m=10, the system is exactly at the deadlock threshold. We need at least m=11 to guarantee one free resource exists.

steps.title: Apply the deadlock-free condition
steps.apply: For no deadlock: m >= 11. Now check the options: A.7 < 11 (unsafe), B.9 < 11 (unsafe), C.10 < 11 (unsafe, just at deadlock threshold), D.13 >= 11 (safe). Therefore m=13 guarantees no deadlock. Note that m=13 = total peak demand, meaning the system can fully satisfy all processes simultaneously.
steps.note: Any m >= 11 actually works. Among the given options, only D.13 satisfies m >= 11. Options A, B, C are all insufficient.

solution.result: m = 13 guarantees deadlock will not occur. Answer: D. 13

questions.formula_note: Deadlock-free condition for single resource type: m >= sum(max_i) - n + 1, equivalently m >= sum(max_i - 1) + 1.

questions.question:
Consider the resource allocation graph in the figure.
A. Find if the system is in a deadlock state.
B. Otherwise, find a safe sequence.

[Resource Allocation Graph: Processes P1, P2, P3, P4; Resources R1 (1 instance), R2 (2 instances), R3 (1 instance), R4 (3 instances).
Assignment edges: R1->P2, R2->P2, R2->P3, R3->P3, R4->P1, R4->P4.
Request edges: P1->R1, P2->R3, P4->R2.]
questions.answer: No deadlock. Safe sequence: P1 -> P4 -> P2 -> P3 (or similar valid sequence)
questions.to_find: Whether the system is in a deadlock state, or a safe sequence if not deadlocked

understand.plain: A Resource Allocation Graph shows which processes are holding which resources and which resources they are waiting for. If there is a cycle with no knot (where all resources in the cycle have only one instance), a deadlock exists. We analyze the graph to detect deadlock or find a safe execution order.

keywords.explain: A directed graph where processes are circles, resources are rectangles with dots (instances). An arrow from resource to process means the resource is assigned to the process; an arrow from process to resource means the process is requesting that resource.
keywords.example: R1->P2 means R1 is currently allocated to P2. P1->R1 means P1 is waiting for R1.

keywords.explain: An ordering of processes such that each process can obtain all remaining needed resources using currently available resources plus resources held by processes that finish before it.
keywords.example: If P1 finishes first and releases R4, then P4 can get R2, finish, and so on.

understand.visual_alt: RAG with 4 processes (P1-P4) and 4 resources. R1 has 1 instance assigned to P2, P1 requests R1. R2 has 2 instances assigned to P2 and P3, P4 requests R2. R3 has 1 instance assigned to P3, P2 requests R3. R4 has 3 instances assigned to P1 and P4.

given.aim: Determine if the resource allocation graph represents a deadlocked state; if not, find a safe sequence.

terms.meaning: Resources not currently allocated to any process. Computed as total instances minus allocated instances.
terms.example: R4 has 3 instances, 2 allocated (to P1 and P4), so 1 available.
terms.connects: Available resources are what can be given to satisfy waiting processes.

terms.meaning: R1(1 instance)->P2, R2(2 instances)->P2 and P3, R3(1 instance)->P3, R4(3 instances)->P1 and P4 (one each, 1 available).
terms.example: P2 currently holds R1 and one instance of R2.
terms.connects: Shows what each process is holding and what the available pool is.

terms.meaning: P1 requests R1, P2 requests R3, P4 requests R2.
terms.example: P4 is blocked waiting for one instance of R2.
terms.connects: Determines which processes are blocked and what they need to proceed.

given.plan: Check if all requests can be satisfied. Find a process whose request can be met with currently available resources. Simulate its completion, release its resources, and repeat — this gives a safe sequence or reveals deadlock.

steps.title: Identify available resources
steps.apply: R1: total=1, allocated=1 (to P2), available=0. R2: total=2, allocated=2 (to P2 and P3), available=0. R3: total=1, allocated=1 (to P3), available=0. R4: total=3, allocated=2 (1 to P1, 1 to P4), available=1.
steps.note: Only R4 has a free instance. No process is requesting R4, so R4's free instance doesn't immediately help.

steps.title: Check which processes can proceed
steps.formula_raw: Process can proceed if: for all resource types, Request_i <= Available
steps.apply: P1 requests R1 (available=0): cannot proceed. P2 requests R3 (available=0): cannot proceed. P3 makes no requests: P3 can proceed! P4 requests R2 (available=0): cannot proceed. P3 is not waiting for any resource. P3 holds R2(1 instance) and R3(1 instance). Let P3 complete and release.
steps.note: P3 has no outstanding requests, so it can run to completion with what it currently holds.

steps.title: After P3 completes, update available
steps.apply: P3 releases: R2(1 instance) and R3(1 instance). New available: R1=0, R2=1, R3=1, R4=1. Now check again: P2 requests R3 (available=1): P2 CAN proceed. P4 requests R2 (available=1): P4 CAN proceed. P1 requests R1 (available=0): still cannot.
steps.note: Multiple processes become unblocked after P3 finishes.

steps.title: Continue: P2 completes
steps.apply: Let P2 proceed (requests R3, available=1). P2 gets R3 and completes. P2 releases: R1(1), R2(1), R3(1). New available: R1=1, R2=2, R3=1, R4=1. Now P1 requests R1 (available=1): P1 CAN proceed. P4 requests R2 (available=2): P4 CAN proceed.
steps.note: After P2 finishes, both P1 and P4 can proceed.

steps.title: P4 and P1 complete
steps.apply: P4 gets R2 and completes, releases R2(1) and R4(1). P1 gets R1 and completes, releases R1(1) and R4(1). All processes complete successfully.
steps.note: Safe sequence: P3 -> P2 -> P4 -> P1 (or P3 -> P2 -> P1 -> P4). The system is NOT in deadlock.

solution.result: The system is NOT in deadlock. A safe sequence is: P3 -> P2 -> P4 -> P1.

questions.formula_note: Resource Allocation Graph analysis and Safety Algorithm: find a process with no unmet requests, simulate its completion, release its resources, and repeat to find a safe sequence or confirm deadlock.

questions.question:
A computer system uses the Banker's Algorithm to deal with deadlocks. Its current state is shown in the table below, where P0, P1, P2 are processes, and R0, R1, R2 are resource types.

Maximum Need:
      R0  R1  R2
P0:    4   1   2
P1:    2   5   1
P2:    1   2   3

Current Allocation:
      R0  R1  R2
P0:    1   0   2
P1:    2   0   3
P2:    2   1   0

Available:
  R0  R1  R2
   2   2   0

A. Show that the system can be in this state for one unit of resource type R1.
B. What will the system do on a request by process P0 for one unit of resource type R1?
questions.answer: The system is in a safe state. On a request by P0 for 1 unit of R1, the Banker's Algorithm grants the request (tentative allocation remains safe: safe sequence P1 -> P0 -> P2 exists).
questions.to_find: Whether the system is in a safe state, and whether a request by P0 for 1 unit of R1 is granted

understand.plain: The Banker's Algorithm is a deadlock avoidance algorithm. Before allocating any resource, it checks if granting the request keeps the system in a 'safe state' — one where all processes can eventually complete. We first verify the current state is safe, then simulate P0's request for R1 and re-check.

keywords.explain: A deadlock avoidance method that only grants resource requests if the resulting state is safe (there exists an ordering of processes where each can get all it needs and finish).
keywords.example: If granting a request leads to a safe state, it is approved; otherwise the process must wait.

keywords.explain: Need[i][j] = Maximum[i][j] - Allocation[i][j]. It represents how many more resources each process may still request.

A system state where there exists at least one safe sequence — an ordering of all processes such that each process's remaining need can be satisfied by the available resources plus resources released by earlier-finishing processes.
keywords.example: If P1 can finish first, releasing its allocations for others to use.

understand.visual_alt: Three processes P0, P1, P2 and three resource types R0, R1, R2. Tables show maximum need, current allocation, and available resources. Need matrix derived by subtracting allocation from maximum.

given.aim: Verify the current state is safe using Banker's Algorithm, and determine if P0's request for 1 unit of R1 can be granted.

terms.meaning: P0 needs: R0=3, R1=1, R2=0. P1 needs: R0=0, R1=5, R2=-2 => Note: Allocation exceeds Max for P1 R2 (2 allocated vs max 1)? Let's re-read from image.
terms.connects: Need determines whether available resources can satisfy a process and let it finish.

terms.meaning: Currently R0 has 2 free, R1 has 2 free, R2 has 0 free instances.
terms.example: Any process needing R2 cannot currently be satisfied.
terms.connects: Available resources are what can be allocated to unblock waiting processes.

terms.meaning: Process P0 is requesting 1 additional unit of resource R1.
terms.example: If granted: P0 allocation becomes (1,1,2), Available becomes (2,1,0).
terms.connects: Need to check if post-grant state is still safe.

given.plan: Step 1: Compute the Need matrix. Step 2: Run the safety algorithm on the current state. Step 3: Simulate P0's request for R1=1, update allocation and available, then re-run the safety algorithm.

steps.title: Compute the Need matrix
steps.apply:
From the image tables:
Max: P0=(4,1,2), P1=(2,5,1), P2=(1,2,3)
Allocation: P0=(1,0,2), P1=(2,0,3), P2=(2,1,0)
Need: P0=(4-1, 1-0, 2-2)=(3,1,0)
      P1=(2-2, 5-0, 1-3)=(0,5,-2) — negative means over-allocated; treat as Need=(0,5,0) in context
      P2=(1-2, 2-1, 3-0)=(-1,1,3) — over-allocated in R0.
Available = (2,2,0).
steps.note: The image shows Allocation P1=(2,0,3) but Max P1=(2,5,1), giving Need P1 R2 = 1-3 = -2 (P1 holds more than its max for R2). This is unusual; the question is about showing the state is valid and finding a safe sequence. We use Need = max(0, Max-Alloc) where needed, or accept the given tables as stated in the problem.

steps.title: Run Safety Algorithm on current state
steps.formula_raw: Find process i where Need[i] <= Available; simulate completion; Available += Allocation[i]; repeat
steps.apply:
Available = (2,2,0).
Check P0: Need=(3,1,0). R0: 3>2 — cannot satisfy.
Check P1: Need=(0,5,0). R1: 5>2 — cannot satisfy.
Check P2: Need=(-1,1,3) => effectively (0,1,3). R2: 3>0 — cannot satisfy.
No process can proceed with R2=0. However, re-reading the image carefully: Available is R0=2, R1=2, R2=0 and the current allocation has P0 holding 2 units of R2. If Max for P2 R2 is 3 and allocation is 0, then need is 3 but available R2=0. The system appears unsafe as stated. The question asks to 'show that the system can be in this state' suggesting it is a valid (possibly unsafe) state, and then asking about P0's request.
steps.note: Given the GATE 1996 context and the question phrasing, the likely intent is: verify current state is safe or note its status, then evaluate the P0 request. The question asks to 'show the system can be in this state' (i.e., it is a valid reachable state), not necessarily that it is safe.

steps.title: Evaluate P0's request for 1 unit of R1
steps.formula_raw: Request[P0] = (0,1,0). Check: Request <= Need[P0] and Request <= Available.
steps.apply: P0's Need for R1 = 1 (from Need matrix). Request = (0,1,0). Check: (0,1,0) <= (3,1,0) [Need] — yes. (0,1,0) <= (2,2,0) [Available] — yes (R1: 1<=2). Pretend to allocate: Available becomes (2,1,0). P0 Allocation becomes (1,1,2). P0 Need becomes (3,0,0). Now run safety check: Check P0: Need=(3,0,0), Available=(2,1,0): R0: 3>2 — no. Check P1: Need=(0,5,0), R1: 5>1 — no. Check P2: Need=(0,1,3), R2: 3>0 — no. Still no safe sequence found. The Banker's Algorithm would deny this request and make P0 wait.
steps.note: Under the Banker's Algorithm, P0's request for R1=1 would be denied because the resulting state is not safe (no safe sequence exists after the tentative allocation).

solution.result: Part A: The system can be in this state (it is a valid reachable configuration). Part B: The Banker's Algorithm will deny P0's request for 1 unit of R1, because granting it leaves the system in an unsafe state (no safe sequence exists).

questions.formula_note: Banker's Algorithm for deadlock avoidance: check Request <= Need and Request <= Available, then perform safety check on the tentative allocation; grant only if a safe sequence exists.
